failure_modes.text: Teams can act on a visible association while missing the underlying causal mechanism that actually needs to change.
failure_modes.source_quote: The most common anti-pattern is mistaking correlation for causation.
failure_modes.mitigation: Trace the causal mechanism: ask what specific process connects the correlated variables and whether intervening on one will actually change the other.

failure_modes.text: Causal claims can become overconfident when the evidence is observational, confounded, or incomplete.
failure_modes.source_quote: Danger when experimentation is impossible but certainty is still claimed.
failure_modes.mitigation: Test the causal claim against confounders: identify at least one plausible alternative explanation and check whether the evidence discriminates between them.

failure_modes.text: Root-cause work can fail when the team stops at the proximate event instead of tracing the machine that produced it.
failure_modes.source_quote: Focusing solely on the case-at-hand (the correlation or outcome) without diagnosing the machine (the causal structure) leads to micromanagement and prevents true learning.
failure_modes.mitigation: Push past the proximate event: use fishbone or five-whys to trace the system that produced the correlation rather than stopping at the visible association.

premortem_questions.text: What mechanism would have to exist for this factor to cause the outcome we claim?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Before one can logically conclude that causation is present, one must explicitly formulate hypotheses about the mechanisms by which one factor causes the other.

premortem_questions.text: Which confounding variables have we identified and measured instead of leaving them to chance?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Analysts must actively identify and measure all potential confounding variables in addition to those specified in the hypotheses to control for spurious correlations.

premortem_questions.text: Are we solving the root cause, or only the symptom that happened immediately before the failure?
premortem_questions.source_quote: This involves striving to separate the proximate cause (what happened immediately before) from the root cause (the true underlying driver).

heuristics.text: Use a fishbone diagram to map multiple candidate causes before committing to a single explanation.
heuristics.source_quote: The Fishbone (or Ishikawa) Diagram is a practical tool used to determine causation more effectively.

heuristics.text: Apply the five whys to move past surface symptoms and keep drilling toward the original driver.
Choose analytical tools based on whether the job is to understand causal drivers or merely to predict a future state.
heuristics.source_quote: The most important defining question at the outset is whether you are primarily trying to understand the drivers of causation ... or if you are primarily trying to predict a future state.

curation_notes.summary: Focused on mechanism tracing, confound control, and root-cause discipline rather than on general research-method survey content.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor analytics sprawl because the raw source is stronger on mechanism hypotheses, fishbone logic, and five-whys diagnosis.
open_questions: The first premortem question is normalized from the hypothesis-and-mechanism framing so it stays reusable beyond research-only wording.

allies: The raw allies section is strong on systems thinking and abductive design work for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonists section explicitly names confirmation bias and fast-thinking distortions, but only the cleanest current-vocabulary ids should survive Wave 3.
structured_tensions: The source is tension-real around prediction versus explanation, but it does not provide a dedicated structured tension block.
